result:
The rain always seems to change the tempo of the city, shifting it from a frantic, staccato rhythm to a prolonged, melancholy legato. When the sky bruises into shades of violet and charcoal, people alter their trajectories. They duck under awnings, quicken their paces, and retreat into the warm, illuminated bellies of cafes and bookstores. I am sitting in one of these cafes right now, nursing a ceramic mug of black coffee that lost its heat twenty minutes ago. The glass pane beside me is fogged at the edges, beaded with heavy droplets that race each other to the windowsill. Through this makeshift cinematic screen, I am watching the world happen. 

There is a word for the specific, dizzying realization that occasionally washes over you when you look out at a crowd. The word is *sonder*. Coined by John Koenig in his *Dictionary of Obscure Sorrows*, it is defined as the profound realization that each random passerby has a life as vivid and complex as your own. Every person you see, no matter how fleetingly they pass through your field of vision, is living an epic story in which they are the undisputed protagonist. They have their own ambitions, their own paralyzing fears, their own inside jokes, their own routines, and their own intricate web of relationships. 

Take, for instance, the older gentleman currently waiting at the crosswalk across the street. He is wearing a meticulously pressed camel-colored overcoat, though his shoulders are stooped under an invisible weight. He holds a brightly colored, floral-patterned umbrella that clashes violently with his austere attire. Is it his? Or did he grab it on his way out the door, a leftover artifact from a spouse who is no longer there? Perhaps he is on his way to visit a grandchild he rarely sees, rehearsing his greetings under his breath. Or maybe he is just a man who appreciates a vibrant umbrella on a dreary Tuesday. I will never know. The light changes to a glowing white silhouette, and he steps off the curb, carrying his entire universe of memories with him, disappearing into the sea of umbrellas.

A few yards behind him, a young woman in her twenties is practically jogging, her boots splashing carelessly into shallow puddles. She is wearing oversized headphones, staring intently at the glowing rectangle of her phone. I can imagine the heavy, driving bass of her music bleeding into the ambient noise of the street, creating a personal soundtrack for her panic. Is she late for a job interview that could alter the trajectory of her career? Is she rushing to the hospital? Or is she simply late for a movie date, annoyed by the unreliable transit system? She brushes past the old man, their shoulders mere inches from colliding. Two distinct universes passing in the void, completely unaware of the infinite complexity of the other. 

Inside the café, the sonder continues. Behind the counter, the barista is absentmindedly wiping down the espresso machine with a damp cloth. It is a repetitive, muscle-memory motion. Her eyes are glazed over, staring out at the same rainy street I am observing. What is she thinking about? The rent that is due in three days? A novel she is struggling to write in the quiet, desperate hours of the morning? A fight she had with her sister last night that left a bitter taste in her mouth? To the man ordering a flat white right now, she is simply an NPC—a non-playable character in the video game of his life, existing solely to provide him with caffeine. But her internal monologue is just as loud, just as urgent, and just as deeply felt as his.

It is a staggering mathematical reality to contemplate. There are eight billion human beings on this planet, which means there are eight billion parallel narratives unfolding concurrently. If you were to map it out, the sheer volume of human experience happening at this exact second would shatter your mind. Right now, someone is taking their very first breath, the cold air rushing into their tiny lungs. Somewhere else, someone is taking their last, leaving behind a room full of weeping relatives or perhaps just the quiet hum of a hospital monitor. Someone is experiencing the exact moment of falling in love, the sudden, terrifying free-fall of realizing they want to merge their life with another. Someone else is having their heart broken in a fluorescent-lit diner, staring at cold french fries and wondering how they are going to survive the night. 

All of this happens simultaneously, wrapped around a floating rock spinning in a silent, apathetic vacuum. We build cities—these massive, towering monuments of steel, glass, and concrete—partly to protect ourselves from the elements, but also, I think, to organize the chaos of all these colliding stories. We stack ourselves in apartment buildings, living vertically, separated from our neighbors by mere inches of drywall. The person sleeping on the other side of your bedroom wall might be crying themselves to sleep, or laughing at a late-night television show, or staring at the ceiling gripped by existential dread. We are so physically close, yet vast oceans of subjective experience keep us fundamentally isolated.

Yet, there is something deeply comforting in this realization. When you are trapped in the claustrophobic echo chamber of your own mind—when your own problems feel insurmountable, when your anxieties scream so loudly you can barely hear yourself think—sonder offers an escape hatch. It is a reminder of our shared insignificance. You are not the center of the universe; you are merely one thread in an unimaginably massive tapestry. Your failures, your embarrassments, and your heartbreaks are not world-ending events; they are simply the standard tax we all pay for the privilege of being human. 

I take a sip of my cold coffee. It is bitter, but it grounds me in the present moment. The rain outside is beginning to let up, the heavy downpour tapering off into a gentle, misty drizzle. The sky is lightening, a pale, bruised yellow bleeding through the grey clouds. People are starting to lower their umbrellas, shaking the water from the canopies like dogs shaking off a bath. 

I look at my reflection in the window glass, superimposed over the bustling street. I realize that to the people outside, looking in, I am just an extra in their movie. I am just "the person drinking coffee by the window." They do not know about my childhood, my deepest regrets, or the words I am stringing together in my head right now. To them, I am a passing shadow, a brief flicker of visual data instantly discarded by their busy minds. 

And that is perfectly fine. It is beautiful, even. We are all wandering through the world, desperately trying to make sense of our own individual plots, occasionally bumping into one another, occasionally sharing a scene, but mostly just walking our own paths. We are alone, together, in the rain. I put my mug down, gather my coat, and step out into the damp street, ready to rejoin the beautiful, chaotic symphony of eight billion moving parts, content to be just another stranger passing by.
